attribution.work: Errol Morris interviewing technique (Interrotron, etc.) + documentary journalism craft
attribution.author: Errol Morris, Marshall Curry, journalistic interview practice (synthesized)
attribution.usage_note: Principles for how interview material was captured and how it should be cut. Some are field-side (what the interviewer should have done); editors inherit those decisions. Most are cut-side: how to use the material you actually have, including the failures.

application_guidance: Use during interview-cut review. These principles diagnose problems specific to talking-head material — questions that produce response-shaped answers, the moment the speaker stops performing, the difference between the answer and the after-answer. If a scene leans on interview, run this checklist.

principles.label: Don't ask the question the answer needs
principles.description: The best interview answer is a complete statement that stands without context. 'Why was Tuesday important?' produces 'Because…' — the answer is a response, not a statement. 'Tell me what happened Tuesday' produces 'On Tuesday I was…' — the answer is now an independent sentence that can be cut into any context. Editors inherit the interviewer's question structure; if the answers are response-shaped, the editing options collapse.
principles.documentary_application: For each interview clip in the cut, ask: does this stand alone, or does it require the question to make sense? If the latter, the clip can only be used in places where the question can be implied — drastically narrowing edit options. When recutting, prioritize clips that are statement-shaped.
review_questions: Does this interview line stand alone, or does it need the question for context?
Are there alternate takes of the same content that are statement-shaped instead of response-shaped?
If the answer starts with 'Yes,' 'No,' 'Because,' or 'Well,' it's response-shaped — is that usable here?

principles.label: Wait one beat after the natural ending
principles.description: The most important sentence often comes after the speaker has 'finished.' A trained interviewer waits one beat in silence after the apparent answer; the speaker often continues with the line they didn't plan to say — the one that's actually true. Editors inherit these moments only if the interviewer left the camera rolling and the speaker was given space.
principles.documentary_application: In the rushes for any important interview moment, check 3–10 seconds AFTER the apparent end of the answer. Is there a line that continues the thought, contradicts it, or qualifies it? Those lines are often the strongest material. They're frequently the line that should be in the cut.
review_questions: What's in the 10 seconds after the apparent end of this answer? Anything stronger than what's in the cut?
Did the speaker say one more thing that the interviewer didn't follow up on?
Is the line I'm using the speaker's planned answer or their actual one?

principles.label: Use the moment they stop performing
principles.description: Interviewees perform for the camera for a while, then forget it's there. The strongest material is often the moment of forgetting — when the answer becomes the actual thought rather than the rehearsed line. Editors can usually identify these moments by tonal shift: voice drops, pace slows, eye contact pattern changes.
principles.documentary_application: For any interviewee who appears in multiple scenes, identify the moment in their footage where they stopped performing. Use the post-performance material wherever possible. If a key claim is delivered in the performing register, look for the same content in the post-performing register.
review_questions: Where in this interview did the speaker stop performing? Is that material in the cut?
Is the current selected line in performing or post-performing register?
Is there an unrehearsed moment of the same content elsewhere in the rushes?

principles.label: One-head fatigue
principles.description: Cutting repeatedly to the same talking head within 30–60 seconds — even with different content — induces audience fatigue. The face becomes a metronome. The cure is variety: different speakers, b-roll, archival, sync footage, observation. A scene with 4 cuts to the same head is structurally identical to a scene with 1 cut to the same head; the extra 3 are decoration.
principles.documentary_application: For each interview-heavy scene, count returns to the same speaker. If a speaker appears 3+ times in 60 seconds, consolidate or replace with b-roll. The exception: emotional escalation — if the speaker is visibly changing across the cuts, the returns earn themselves. Otherwise they're filler.
review_questions: How many times does this scene cut back to the same head in 60 seconds?
Is each return earning itself — is the speaker visibly changing, or is the content just continuing?
Could 2 returns be consolidated into 1 longer take with b-roll under it?

principles.label: Put the question inside the answer
principles.description: When the interviewer's question is necessary for context, the best answers absorb the question into the answer's first clause. 'When the decision came down, I was…' is the answer that contains the question. Editors can engineer this in two ways: pick rushes takes where the speaker did this naturally, or sequence the cut so a previous scene supplies the question implicitly.
principles.documentary_application: Audit interview lines for clarity: does the line tell the audience what was being asked, or do you need to insert a card / VO / prior context to set up the answer? If the latter, look for an alternative take that absorbs the question, OR put the answer in a context where the question is implicit from what just happened.
review_questions: Could this interview answer be inserted into a new context without losing meaning?
Does the line absorb the question, or does it require external setup?
Is there a take where the speaker self-contextualizes more naturally?
